preamble: The transformation from a voiceless dental fricative to an alveolar flap is a form of rhotacism or allophonic competition, often passing through a stop stage [t] as observed in certain English dialects.

examples.note: In many dialects, /r/ after a dental fricative is realized as a flap. Furthermore, in dialects with Th-stopping (θ > t), the resulting [t] can then flap (t > ɾ) in appropriate environments.
Dentalized environments often show a close relationship between the dental fricative and the following rhotic realization.

sources: Wells, J. C. (1982). Accents of English.
Hickey, R. (2007). Irish English: History and Present-Day Forms.
